dimensions.label: Research craft
dimensions.description: Comfort designing studies, recruiting, and analyzing qualitative + mixed data.

dimensions.label: Pace & ambiguity
dimensions.description: Tolerance for 2-week timelines and decisions made on partial evidence.

dimensions.label: Product sense
dimensions.description: Ability to connect research to design, engineering, and business decisions.

dimensions.label: Communication
dimensions.description: Writing tight readouts and presenting to non-researchers.

dimensions.label: Stakeholder fluency
dimensions.description: Working with PMs, designers, execs, and saying no without burning bridges.

questions.prompt: I can plan a generative interview study (n≈8) and have a synthesis draft within two weeks.
I am comfortable running a usability test with think-aloud protocol.
I can write a one-page research plan with clear questions, method, and decisions it will inform.
I can analyze mixed qual/quant data and resist over-interpreting small samples.
I am comfortable shipping insights that are 80% confident if the decision is reversible.
I can re-scope a study mid-flight when the product question changes.
I would rather deliver a useful answer in two weeks than a perfect one in two months.
I can articulate how a research insight changes a roadmap, a metric, or a screen.
I read product specs, prototypes, or analytics dashboards without getting lost.
I can identify when research is the wrong tool and recommend something cheaper.
I think in terms of users, jobs-to-be-done, and trade-offs — not only populations and effects.
I can write a one-page readout that a busy PM will actually read.
I am comfortable presenting findings to skeptical engineers or executives.
I can replace academic hedging with plain claims while preserving honest uncertainty.
I can disagree with a designer or PM and still get invited to the next kickoff.
I can say no to a research request and offer a useful alternative.
I can read the politics of a room and adjust how I deliver bad news.
I am energized — not drained — by working with non-researchers every day.

bands.label: Not yet
bands.blurb: You have real research instincts, but the pace and politics of UXR will frustrate you today. Use the next 6 months to ship one small applied study.

bands.label: Curious
bands.blurb: You'd survive a UXR role with deliberate practice. Focus on speed, product framing, and short readouts.

bands.label: Strong fit
bands.blurb: Your craft transfers cleanly. Spend your effort on UXR portfolio artifacts and stakeholder examples.

bands.label: Ready to apply
bands.blurb: On paper you're already a UXR. The remaining gap is positioning — your CV, samples, and interview stories.
